section.title: The Ten Commandments Restated
section.sectionRationale: As the covenant renewal proper begins, Moses restates the Ten Commandments given at Horeb to the generation now poised to enter the land, grounding the whole book's statutes and ordinances in these same ten words spoken by God Himself.
section.thesis: Standing at the border of the land, Moses restates the Ten Commandments given at Horeb to the new generation, reminding them that the LORD spoke these words directly, face to face, and that Israel's own trembling request for a mediator reveals both God's holiness and their need for someone to stand between them and Him.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: the covenant made at Horeb, with this generation; no other gods and no idols; the name of the LORD; the Sabbath, grounded in deliverance from slavery; honoring father and mother; the five commands concerning neighbor; and the people's fear and their request for a mediator.

units.label: The covenant made at Horeb, with us

verses.text: Then Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: Hear, O Israel, the statutes and ordinances that I declare in your hearing this day. Learn them and observe them carefully.
The LORD our God made a covenant with us at Horeb.
He did not make this covenant with our fathers, but with all of us who are alive here today.
The LORD spoke with you {{panim|face to face}} out of the fire on the mountain.
At that time I was standing between the LORD and you to declare to you the word of the LORD, because you were afraid of the fire and would not go up the mountain. And He said:
“I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
You shall have no other gods before Me.
You shall not make for yourself an idol in the form of anything in the heavens above, on the earth below, or in the waters beneath.

You shall not bow down to them or worship them; for I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on their children to the third and fourth generations of those who hate Me,
but showing loving devotion to a thousand generations of those who love Me and keep My commandments.
You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not leave anyone unpunished who takes His name in vain.

units.label: The Sabbath, grounded in deliverance
units.note: remember that you were a slave

verses.text: Observe the Sabbath day by keeping it holy, as the LORD your God has commanded you.
Six days you shall labor and do all your work,

but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God, on which you must not do any work, neither you, nor your son or daughter, nor your manservant or maidservant, nor your ox or donkey or any of your livestock, nor the foreigner within your gates, so that your manservant and maidservant may rest as you do.

Remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and that the LORD your God brought you out of there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm. That is why the LORD your God has commanded you to keep the Sabbath day.

units.label: Honor father and mother
units.note: so that it may go well with you

verses.text: Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God has commanded you, so that your days may be long and that it may go well with you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.
You shall not murder.
You shall not commit adultery.
You shall not steal.
You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.

You shall not {{chamad|covet}} your neighbor's wife. You shall not covet your neighbor's house or field, or his manservant or maidservant, or his ox or donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor.”

units.label: The people's fear, and a mediator

verses.text: The LORD spoke these commandments in a loud voice to your whole assembly out of the fire, the cloud, and the deep darkness on the mountain; He added nothing more. And He wrote them on two tablets of stone and gave them to me.
And when you heard the voice out of the darkness while the mountain was blazing with fire, all the heads of your tribes and your elders approached me,
and you said, “Behold, the LORD our God has shown us His glory and greatness, and we have heard His voice out of the fire. Today we have seen that a man can live even if God speaks with him.
But now, why should we die? For this great fire will consume us, and we will die, if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any longer.
For who of all flesh has heard the voice of the living God speaking out of the fire, as we have, and survived?
Go near and listen to all that the LORD our God says. Then you can tell us everything the LORD our God tells you; we will listen and obey.”
And the LORD heard the words you spoke to me, and He said to me, “I have heard the words that these people have spoken to you. They have done well in all that they have spoken.
If only they had such a heart to fear Me and keep all My commandments always, so that it might be well with them and with their children forever.
Go and tell them: ‘Return to your tents.’
But you stand here with Me, that I may speak to you all the commandments and statutes and ordinances you are to teach them to follow in the land that I am giving them to possess.”
So be careful to do as the LORD your God has commanded you; you are not to turn aside to the right or to the left.
You must walk in all the ways that the LORD your God has commanded you, so that you may live and prosper and prolong your days in the land that you will possess.

panim.gloss: Face to face. Describes the directness and intimacy of the LORD's speech at Horeb, not that Israel literally saw God's form, but that His words came without any human intermediary, making Israel's request for a mediator in this same chapter all the more striking.

chamad.gloss: To covet, to desire strongly, often with a view to taking. Unlike the other nine commands, which address outward acts, this one names the internal desire itself as sin, addressing the heart's longing before it ever becomes an action.

basic.context: Moses restates the Ten Commandments to the generation about to enter the land, reminding them that these words were spoken directly by God at Horeb to their own generation, not merely to their parents, and that this same generation once begged for a mediator because God's direct voice terrified them.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historical covenant renewal, restating unchanging commands for a new audience. Christians today are not bound to Israel's specific national covenant form, but the moral substance, love for God and neighbor, remains the abiding, unchanging expression of God's character.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD spoke panim el panim, face to face, meaning directly and without a human intermediary, which makes Israel's fear and their request for Moses to mediate all the more significant. The tenth command addresses chamad, covetous desire itself, naming an inward longing as sin before it ever becomes an outward act.
basic.typology: Israel's terror before God's direct voice and their request for Moses to stand between them and God anticipates the greater mediation of Christ, who alone can bring sinners into God's presence without terror, Hebrews 12:18-24.
basic.christ: Hebrews 12:18-24 draws directly on this scene, contrasting the terrifying, untouchable mountain of Sinai with the access believers now have to God through Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, whose blood speaks a better word than the fear this chapter records.
basic.god: God is holy enough that His own redeemed people cannot bear the sound of His unmediated voice, yet He is also relational enough to commend their fear as 'doing well' and to long for a heart in them that would keep His commandments forever out of love, not merely terror.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 20:1-21 records the first giving of these same commandments at Horeb. Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts the terror of Sinai with the access believers now have through Christ. Matthew 22:37-40 summarizes the whole law as love for God and neighbor.
basic.oneStory: God gives His unchanging moral law directly to His redeemed people, and their instinctive fear before His unmediated voice reveals humanity's deep need for a mediator. That need is finally met in Jesus Christ, the one mediator who brings God's people near without terror and writes this same law on their hearts.

context.literary: This chapter opens the covenant-renewal core of Deuteronomy, restating the Decalogue almost word for word from Exodus 20 before Moses expands on its implications in the chapters that follow, especially the great command of chapter 6.
context.historical: Because the generation that stood at Horeb had died in the wilderness, Moses stresses in verse 3 that this covenant is made with 'all of us who are alive here today', a deliberate move to keep the new generation from treating the law as belonging only to their parents.
context.canonical: These ten words remain the moral backbone of the whole Bible's ethical teaching, echoed by the prophets, summarized by Jesus, and woven into the New Testament's vision of a life renewed by grace rather than mere external conformity.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press upon the new generation that the covenant made at Horeb belongs to them personally, not merely to their ancestors, and to root their obedience in gratitude for a redemption already accomplished.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes Israel's covenant obligations under the old covenant given at Sinai, addressed to a specific historical nation, while its moral core carries forward as abiding truth fulfilled and reissued for all people under the new covenant in Christ.

debates.question: Why does Deuteronomy ground the Sabbath command in deliverance from Egypt (v.15) while Exodus 20:11 grounds it in God's rest after creation?

views.label: Complementary reasons for one command
views.case: Both motives are true and were likely taught together; Exodus highlights creation's pattern of rest while Deuteronomy adds the redemption motive fitting for a generation who must remember they were once slaves.

views.label: The emphasis shifts to suit the audience and purpose
views.case: Deuteronomy is addressed to a generation about to extend Sabbath rest to their own servants and foreigners, so Moses appropriately foregrounds the redemption motive: since you were slaves and were freed, extend that same freedom rhythm to those under your care.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold the Sabbath as reflecting God's character in both creation and redemption; the difference lies only in which true motive Moses chooses to highlight for this specific pastoral moment.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD spoke panim el panim, face to face, directly to Israel at Horeb, without a human intermediary, which makes their trembling request for Moses to mediate in this same chapter deeply significant. The tenth command targets chamad, coveting, naming an inward desire itself as sin rather than only the outward theft or violence it might produce.

typology.type: Israel's terror before God's unmediated voice at Horeb
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the need for a perfect mediator, fulfilled in Christ, who brings believers into God's presence without terror, Hebrews 12:18-24.

typology.type: Moses standing between the LORD and the people (v.5)
typology.fulfillment: A shadow of Christ's greater mediation as the one mediator between God and mankind, 1 Timothy 2:5.

study.christ: Hebrews 12:18-24 explicitly contrasts this terrifying scene, a blazing mountain, a voice the people begged to stop hearing, with the access believers now enjoy through Jesus, 'the mediator of a new covenant, whose sprinkled blood speaks a better word than the blood of Abel.' Where Israel needed Moses to stand between them and God, believers now come near through Christ Himself.
study.god: God is holy enough that His own redeemed people cannot bear His unmediated voice, and He honors their fear, calling it something they 'did well' to feel, verse 28, since a right fear of God is itself a form of wisdom. Yet He also expresses deep relational longing, wishing they had hearts that would keep His commands out of love, not only fear, forever.

crossReferences.note: The original giving of these same Ten Commandments at Horeb, which this chapter restates for the new generation.
Contrasts the terror of Sinai directly with the access believers now have to God through Christ, the mediator of a new covenant.
Names Christ as the one mediator between God and mankind, fulfilling what Moses could only foreshadow.
Jesus summarizes the whole law, including these ten commands, as love for God and love for neighbor.

study.oneStory: God gives His unchanging moral law directly to His redeemed people, and their instinctive terror before His unmediated voice exposes humanity's deep need for someone to stand between them and a holy God. That need is finally and fully met in Jesus Christ, the true mediator who brings God's people near without fear and writes this same law on hearts renewed by His Spirit.

sermon.title: God's Law
sermon.why: Keller preaches through this restatement of the Ten Commandments, drawing out how the law reveals both the holiness of God and humanity's need for a mediator, pointing forward to the grace that alone can produce heartfelt obedience.

quiz.q: According to verse 3, with whom did the LORD make the covenant at Horeb?

options.text: With this very generation, alive and present, not only with their ancestors.
options.note: Verse 3 states this explicitly, pressing the covenant's claim onto the new generation directly.

options.text: Only with the generation that had already died in the wilderness.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly denies this, saying the covenant was made with those alive that very day.

options.text: Only with Moses personally.
options.note: The text says the covenant was made with the whole assembly, not Moses alone.

options.text: The verse makes no claim about who the covenant was made with.
options.note: Verse 3 makes a specific and deliberate claim about this.

quiz.q: What reason does Deuteronomy 5:15 give for keeping the Sabbath, distinct from the reason given in Exodus 20:11?

options.text: Remembering that they were slaves in Egypt and that the LORD delivered them with a mighty hand.
options.note: Verse 15 grounds the Sabbath in redemption from slavery, complementing Exodus 20's emphasis on creation.

options.text: Because the seventh day was considered unlucky for travel.
options.note: No such superstition appears; the stated reasoning is theological, tied to the exodus from Egypt.

options.text: Because livestock needed constant labor and could not rest.
options.note: The text specifies rest for livestock as part of the command, the opposite of this option.

options.text: No reason at all is given for the Sabbath in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 15 gives an explicit, stated reason.

quiz.q: How did the people respond to hearing the LORD's voice directly from the fire (vv.24-27)?

options.text: They were afraid they would die and asked Moses to hear from God and relay His words to them instead.
options.note: Verses 25-27 record exactly this fearful request for a mediator.

options.text: They demanded to hear more directly from God, not less.
options.note: The opposite occurred; they wanted less direct exposure to God's voice, not more.

options.text: They ignored the experience entirely.
options.note: Their response is described in vivid, specific detail, not ignored.

options.text: They rejected Moses as their spokesman.
options.note: They specifically asked Moses to be their spokesman, the opposite of rejecting him.

quiz.q: How did the LORD respond to Israel's request for a mediator (vv.28-29)?

options.text: He said they had spoken well, and wished they had a heart to fear Him and keep His commands always.
options.note: Verses 28-29 record God's approving response along with His longing for their lasting faithfulness.

options.text: He rebuked them for their fear as a lack of faith.
options.note: The text records approval of their request, not rebuke.

options.text: He refused to speak to Moses any further.
options.note: The opposite happens; God continues speaking directly to Moses for the people's benefit.

options.text: No response from the LORD is recorded here.
options.note: Verses 28-29 record a clear, specific divine response.

quiz.q: Which commandment addresses an inward desire rather than only an outward act?

options.text: The tenth commandment, against coveting.
options.note: Verse 21 names coveting, an internal desire, as the object of this command.

options.text: The sixth commandment, against murder.
options.note: Murder is an outward act; coveting is the command that specifically targets internal desire.

options.text: The third commandment, about the LORD's name.
options.note: This concerns speech and use of God's name, not an internal desire as such.

options.text: None of the ten commandments address internal desire.
options.note: The tenth commandment explicitly does, naming coveting itself as the sin.

questions: Moses insisted this covenant belonged to the present generation, not just their ancestors. Do you relate to God's commands as truly your own calling today, or as something belonging to a past generation of faith?
Israel's fear of God's direct voice led them to ask for a mediator. How does knowing Christ is now your mediator change the way you approach God, compared to approaching Him on your own?
God said He wished Israel had a heart to keep His commands always, not just in a moment of fear. Where is your obedience currently driven by fear rather than by a heart that loves Him?

The tenth commandment addresses coveting, a desire of the heart before it ever becomes an action. What desire are you nursing right now that has not yet become an outward act, but is already shaping your heart?
The Sabbath command in this chapter is grounded in remembering slavery and deliverance. What rhythms of rest do you need to reclaim as an act of remembering what God has delivered you from?

translationNotes.note: Unlike Exodus 20:11, which grounds the Sabbath in God's rest after creation, this restatement grounds it in Israel's deliverance from slavery in Egypt, adding a redemption motive appropriate for a covenant-renewal generation.
The order of the tenth commandment differs slightly from Exodus 20:17: here the neighbor's wife is named first, before house and field, a variation in emphasis rather than a change in meaning.

'A man can live even if God speaks with him' reflects the ancient assumption that direct exposure to God's holiness was normally fatal, making the people's survival itself a wonder, and their fear understandable.
